title: Soil pH Calculator: Lime & Sulfur 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Soil pH Calculator: How to Correct Acidity or Alkalinity
description: Calculate how much lime or sulfur to add for soil pH correction. Get results in kg/m² based on current and target pH. Free, no signup.
seoKeywords: how much lime per square meter
intro: **Soil pH** directly affects nutrient availability for plants. Most vegetables thrive at **pH 6.0–7.0**. If your soil is too **acidic** (low pH), add **lime**. If it's too **alkaline** (high pH), use **elemental sulfur** or iron sulfate. This calculator tells you how many **kg per m²** of amendment you need.
keyTakeaway: **Acidic soil (pH < 6)**: apply agricultural lime, ~100–200 g/m² for each 0.5 point of pH rise. **Alkaline soil (pH > 7.5)**: apply sulfur, ~30–50 g/m² for each 0.5 point of pH drop.
useCases: You tested your soil pH and it's outside the ideal range.
You want to grow blueberries (pH 4.5–5.5 preferred) but your soil is too alkaline.
You need to calculate how much lime to buy for a large garden area.
You want to adjust hydrangea pH to shift flower colors from pink to blue (or vice versa).
You're preparing soil for a new vegetable garden.

fields.label: Soil type
fields.help: Sandy soils need less product; clay soils need more.

faq.q: What's the best way to test soil pH?
faq.a: Use an inexpensive home pH kit ($3–8 from garden centers) or a digital pH meter ($10–20). For the most accurate results, send a soil sample to your county extension office—most offer testing for $10–25 and test pH, nutrients, organic matter, and more.

faq.q: What type of lime should I use?
faq.a: Agricultural limestone (calcium carbonate) is the safest and most common choice for gardens. Dolomitic limestone adds magnesium if your soil is deficient. Hydrated lime works faster but can burn plants—it's best for professionals only.

faq.q: How long does lime take to raise soil pH?
faq.a: Agricultural limestone takes 2–3 months to noticeably raise pH, which is why it's best applied in fall for spring planting. Hydrated lime works within weeks but is more aggressive and harder to control.

faq.q: How much sulfur does it take to lower soil pH?
faq.a: Elemental sulfur works at about 30–50 g/m² per 0.5 pH point drop, but it takes 3–4 months and works best in warm soil. Aluminum sulfate acts faster (within weeks) but is harsher on the soil ecosystem.

faq.q: Can I use vinegar to lower soil pH?
faq.a: Vinegar and coffee grounds temporarily acidify the soil solution around roots, but they don't permanently change soil pH. For lasting results, add elemental sulfur, which chemically converts to sulfuric acid in soil.

faq.q: What soil pH do common vegetables need?
faq.a: Most vegetables prefer pH 6.0–7.0. Exceptions: potatoes prefer 5.0–6.0 (to reduce scab), blueberries need 4.5–5.5, and asparagus likes 6.5–7.5. Outside these ranges, nutrients lock up even if present in the soil.

faq.q: Does adding compost change soil pH?
faq.a: Mature compost acts as a buffer—it pulls acidic soil toward neutral and slightly lowers alkaline soil. Adding compost regularly (1–2 inches annually) is the best way to gradually stabilize pH in the 6–7 range.

faq.q: When should I apply lime—spring or fall?
faq.a: Apply lime in fall (September–November) so it has 2–3 months to work before spring planting. If you need faster results, use hydrated lime in early spring, though it's less predictable and harder to control.

faq.q: Can I adjust pH in raised beds or containers?
faq.a: Yes, but it's easier to start with the right mix: use an acidic base (sulfur, peat moss) or alkaline base (limestone) in your potting soil. For existing beds, add sulfur to acidify or crushed limestone to raise pH.

explanation:
## Why Soil pH Matters

Soil pH determines which nutrients are available to plants. When pH is outside the 6.0–7.5 range, phosphorus, iron, manganese, and zinc can become locked up even though they're present in the soil. **Correcting pH before fertilizing multiplies the effect of your fertilizer.**

## Optimal pH by Crop Type

| Crop | Target pH |
|---|---|
| Blueberries | 4.5–5.5 |
| Potatoes | 5.0–6.0 |
| Blue hydrangeas | 5.0–5.5 |
| Tomatoes | 6.0–7.0 |
| Lettuce/arugula | 6.0–7.0 |
| Lawn grass | 6.0–7.0 |
| Alfalfa | 6.8–7.5 |
| Asparagus | 6.5–7.5 |

## What to Apply Based on Soil pH

- **Raise pH (acidic to neutral)**: calcium carbonate (agricultural lime) or dolomite limestone. Standard rate: **100 g/m² per 0.5 pH unit** in loam soil.
- **Lower pH (alkaline to acidic)**: elemental sulfur or aluminum sulfate. Sulfur: **50–80 g/m² per 0.5 pH unit**.

## When to Apply & Common Mistakes

- **Sandy soils** need 30% less product than loam.
- **Clay/heavy soils** need 30–50% more.
- **Incorporate with a rake**—don't just sprinkle.
- **Wait 2–3 months** before testing pH again.
- For planting dates, see our [seeding depth calculator](/calculator-seeding-depth-seed).

example.title: Real example: 10 m² garden raising pH from 5.5 to 6.5
steps: **Inputs**: current pH = 5.5, target pH = 6.5, area = 10 m², loam soil.
**Application**: spread evenly, work into soil 2–4 inches with a rake, water, wait 2–3 months.
example.result: You need 2 kg of agricultural limestone to raise pH by 1 point in 10 m² of loam soil.

sources.name: INTA — National Institute of Agricultural Technology
